content: Primary brain tumors arise from CNS cells; ~30,000 new cases/year in US. Classification by cell of origin: gliomas (astrocytoma, oligodendroglioma, ependymoma), meningiomas (from arachnoid cap cells), pituitary adenomas, schwannomas, medulloblastomas. WHO 2021 classification integrates histology with molecular markers. Glioblastoma (GBM, WHO grade 4) is most common malignant primary brain tumor in adults—median survival 15 months despite treatment. IDH mutation status is key prognostic factor: IDH-mutant gliomas have better prognosis than IDH-wildtype. 1p/19q codeletion defines oligodendroglioma (better prognosis, chemo-sensitive). MGMT promoter methylation predicts temozolomide response in GBM. Metastatic brain tumors (from lung, breast, melanoma, renal, colon) are more common than primary. Symptoms depend on location: headache (worse in morning, Valsalva), seizures, focal deficits, cognitive changes, increased ICP (nausea, vomiting, papilledema).
